coredata.dc:title: Hybrid adaptive battery parameter estimation approach for equivalent circuit model toolbox
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Najafi, Amin
Masih-Tehrani, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: This article presents a software tool for estimating the equivalent circuit model (ECM) of lithium-ion batteries using battery voltage and current datasets based on dynamic and static RC loop curve fitting techniques. This tool calculates the state of charge (SOC) of a battery and clusters experimental data based on the SOC. This provides the possibility of determining the equivalent circuit of the battery. Also, by leveraging the unique structure of the equivalent circuit, the method of estimating the effectiveness of this tool is integrated. The supplementary idea of this article is based on the calculation of the best method of estimating the equivalent circuit based on the hybrid adaptive battery parameter estimation (HABPE) based on model architecture and fitting it accordingly. By comparing the data from the extracted model with the experimental data of the battery, an accuracy of 82–87 % has been achieved, depending on the type of battery model. This method provides an easy and accurate approach to obtaining battery parameters and constructing its equivalent circuit for future applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Equivalent circuit model (ECM)
State of charge
Battery parameter estimation (BPE)
Hybrid adaptive battery parameter estimation (HABPE)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the battery equivalent circuit model extraction methodology. (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).

Voltage and Current profiles. a: LA92 Driving cycle (Panasonic Dataset), b: NASA Dataset, c: Experiment Dataset. (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).
Evaluation of battery cell parameter curve fitting methods. (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).

Fitted diagrams of battery parameters. a: Battery open circuit voltage, b: cell internal resistance c: cell dynamic loop resistance, d: cell dynamic loop capacity. (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).

Voltage Comparison in. a: Panasonic LA92 Cycle, c: Panasonic UDDS Cycle, e: NASA, g: Experiment, T = 25 °C (Static Mode). b: Panasonic LA92 Cycle, d: Panasonic UDDS Cycle, f: NASA, h: Experiment, T = 25 °C (Dynamic Mode). (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).

Method Comparison in. a: Smooth current profile (LA92), b: Drastic current profile (LA92). c: Hybrid method (UDDS), d: Hybrid method (LA92). (For interpretation of the reference to colour in the text, the reader is referred to web version of this article).

list-item.para: Require a mass of tests to model diffusion resistance
Increase computational cost and complexity in SOx estimation
Augment OCV-SOC curve robustness against temperature, hysteresis, ageing
Simple structure and low model complexity
Require a mass of tests to capture parameter dependencies

row.entry: 2-RC fractional-order model with a Warburg element [20]

Estimated numerical data of battery equivalent circuit at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Hybrid adaptive battery parameter estimation approach for equivalent circuit model toolbox

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive Engineering, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Automotive Engineering, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article presents a software tool for estimating the equivalent circuit model (ECM) of lithium-ion batteries using battery voltage and current datasets based on dynamic and static RC loop curve fitting techniques. This tool calculates the state of charge (SOC) of a battery and clusters experimental data based on the SOC. This provides the possibility of determining the equivalent circuit of the battery. Also, by leveraging the unique structure of the equivalent circuit, the method of estimating the effectiveness of this tool is integrated. The supplementary idea of this article is based on the calculation of the best method of estimating the equivalent circuit based on the hybrid adaptive battery parameter estimation (HABPE) based on model architecture and fitting it accordingly. By comparing the data from the extracted model with the experimental data of the battery, an accuracy of 82–87 % has been achieved, depending on the type of battery model. This method provides an easy and accurate approach to obtaining battery parameters and constructing its equivalent circuit for future applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

row.entry: Permanent link to code/repository used for this code version
Software code languages, tools and services used
Compilation requirements, operating environments and dependencies
If available, link to developer documentation/manual

ce:section.ce:para: The energy storage systems field is vital for electric vehicles and related [1]. Lithium-ion batteries are favoured for their role in battery-based system design [2]. Precise modelling of cell-level equations is crucial for accurate energy system modelling [3]. Yet, battery experiments can be complex and expensive, leading to the use of equivalent circuits for behaviour simulation [4]. Battery models fall into three categories: electrochemical, electrical equivalent circuits, and data-driven [5,6]. Electrochemical models involve intricate chemical interactions [7]. However, these models are highly complex and time-consuming [8]. Models based on experimental data are obtained by conducting experimental tests on real cells and are generalized to all functional conditions of cells using machine learning and artificial intelligence methods [9]. Electrical equivalent circuit models are popular due to their simplicity and real-time applications [10]. These models simulate battery behaviour using electrical implementation [11]. Table 1 shows the strategies available in 1RC and multi-RC circuit loops.

Battery ECM includes internal resistance and open circuit voltage as the main elements of static behaviour [23]. To simulate the dynamic behaviour of the battery during charging and discharging, the circuit also includes loops consisting of a capacitor and parallel resistance, as shown in Fig. 1 . Of course, it is very important that despite the existence of multi-loop dynamic models with more electrical characteristics, single-loop models are more popular and have appropriate accuracy and lower computational and implementation costs. The values of these electrical elements are determined using battery testing devices, which may not always be accessible [24]. Hence, this article employs a straightforward approach to compute the battery's equivalent circuit by utilizing its charge and discharge data across various operational cycles [25,26]. This technique permits the generation of diverse simulated battery cell models, facilitating the examination of battery pack performance under varying test conditions [27]. This article simulates the behaviour of the battery based on the fitting of the estimated data of the equivalent circuit of the battery, obtained from the clustering of the battery data, and is a suitable representative of its changes. The most important advantage of this method is the lack of laboratory model extraction methods. The usefulness of the method is to avoid the use of heavy calculations and to fit the curve based on experimental clustered data. Also, another benefit is the possibility of personalisation in the battery and different conditions based on the researcher's needs.

Previous studies in this field have typically been case-by-case and rely on optimization methods such as genetics [28], artificial intelligence and machine learning methods [11], advanced deep learning methods such as LSTM [29], or Feed forward intelligent networks [30], and model-based methods such as Kalan filter [31] to extract battery parameters and form a circuit [32]. In contrast, this study strives to employ a universal approach to compute battery parameters solely using available battery voltage and current data [33,34]. This methodology offers researchers a convenient and reasonably precise avenue to acquire battery parameters and establish its equivalent circuit for future endeavours. An essential innovation lies in deriving battery behaviour from the charge/discharge current profile and fitting battery identifier data across distinct conditions without necessitating laboratory method calibration. Additionally, HABPE dynamically adapts the parameter estimation method by considering battery current profiles and selecting the battery model architecture, amalgamating these factors to accommodate battery performance conditions. Amongst the main innovations and achievements of this research, the following can be mentioned:
•
Utilizing diverse equivalent battery model architectures and estimation techniques, including simple static and comprehensive dynamic modes.
•
Prioritizing estimation efficiency by analysing battery power fluctuations.
•
Offering an accurate estimated model derived from primary battery data, obviating the requirement for specialized testing.
•
Identifying the optimal parameter estimation technique based on equivalent battery model output data through the HABPE method.

This article utilizes experimental data from a lithium-ion battery, including its voltage and current during various driving cycles, to extract charge and discharge profiles and calculate the state of charge (SOC) throughout the cycle. By fitting curves in different states, the static/dynamic elements of the equivalent circuit model of the battery are determined, and the battery model is extracted based on these elements. For the convenience of the user, it is possible to determine the battery parameters via GUI by determining the simple BPE and HABPE methods and the possibility of having a battery equivalent circuit. The performance of the estimation tool is evaluated by comparing the extracted model data with experimental battery data. Fig. 1 illustrates the steps taken in this study to develop a predictive battery model.

In this article, a software tool is proposed that takes as input a MATLAB database file (.mat file) containing time, terminal voltage, and battery current data. The software tool then performs an initial review of the available data and calculates the battery's state of charge (SOC) at any given moment using the nominal capacity of the cell and its instantaneous current. This is done using Eq. (1) [35].
(1)

represents its instantaneous current at a given time. The article employs Kirchhoff equations to estimate static parameters like open circuit voltage and internal resistance of the battery's equivalent circuit. This approach employs an RC circuit loop, described by Eq. (2) [36].
(2)

It should be noted that in Eq. (1) and static mode, the value of
V 1
is considered to be equal to zero, and the circuit is modelled with an internal resistance. In dynamic mode, the RC loop shown in Fig. 1 is added to the circuit, and Eq. (3) is also used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Software function based on simple BPE and HABPE
ce:section.ce:para: In this article, an illustrative example is presented using the data of a Panasonic 18,650 cylindrical lithium-ion battery. The dataset [33] includes data from five different ambient temperatures and 10 standard driving cycles. Also, to guarantee the generality of the method, 2 other datasets have been used to test the tool. In this paper, Panasonic data are used for training and testing, and the NASA dataset [38], and experimental battery data [39] are used. Attempts have been made to include battery variations, conditions, etc. in this data. The complete specifications of the datasets are presented in Table 2 .

In this article, curve fitting based on Eq. (2) with the value of V1 = 0 is used to extract the behaviour based on the internal resistance and the open circuit voltage in the static state. For the dynamic state and the addition of dynamic behaviour loop components, Eq. (4) is used.
(4)

Of course, it should be mentioned that as the final use of this tool, the specific battery data can be used in a very simple way to extract the equivalent circuit for study. For this purpose, an experimental set-up is needed, including a data storage device for battery terminal voltage and current. For this, common battery testing devices (with storage) or a personal log board can be used with appropriate accuracy, sufficient charge and discharge current should be given to the battery randomly (preferably according to a standard test) and its voltage behaviour data per unit of operating time. and enter the tool. Fig. 2 in the article portrays the voltage and current profiles of the battery during the discharge cycle, particularly in the LA92 driving cycle. The LA92 cycle is characterized by a maximum discharge current of 10 A, and within this cycle, the battery's discharge extends to its cut-off voltage in roughly 230 min. These datasets serve as training data for the developed Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM). Also, the figure represents the charge and discharge profiles in the battery data of experimental and laboratory datasets. As you can see, the effort is to cover slow currents and strong fluctuations in all types of batteries with different voltage ranges. Of course, another important point is the use of battery pack data (including 4 cells) [40] in experimental data. In this way, the interaction and effect of cells on each other is also considered.

Experimental data from the dataset was used to extract current and terminal voltage values, which were then fitted with linear and 5th-degree polynomial curves to obtain battery parameters. Internal resistance and open circuit voltage were modelled against charge level. Multiple curve fitting methods were employed to enhance accuracy. Table 3 details the outcomes of various methods for open circuit voltage (OCV) and internal resistance (R0) evaluation at T = 25 °C.

The polynomial fitting method outperforms others, becoming the software's default choice. Fig. 3 displays method comparisons and fitting errors. The software provides battery-equivalent circuit model estimates through two curve-fitting stages. The subsequent analysis focuses on Panasonic dataset results.

According to the determination of battery model estimation and extraction functions, the equivalent circuit according to current input conditions can be considered in a simple method (both static and dynamic), or as a combination of two methods (HABPE) in different conditions, which is considered according to the switch The architecture is estimated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Software results and discussion on simple method
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, to evaluate the data extracted from the software, the results of the Panasonic dataset are reviewed. To investigate the effect of ambient temperature on the performance of the software, the extracted data of the battery model for the LA92 driving cycle was checked and the temperature of 25 ° is given in Table 4 . Other temperatures)10 and −20 °C (had similar behaviour.

At lower temperatures, cells exhibit reduced
V OCV
and reach maximum cut-off voltage earlier due to lower R0 at higher temperatures, resulting in higher voltage. This aligns with prior studies and supports parameter estimation accuracy. Table 5 displays estimated values at 25 °C and 2 driving cycles, illustrating the impact of driving cycles on cell parameters at a constant temperature.

The data in Table 5 indicate that there is a small variation in the equivalent circuit parameters of the battery for different driving cycles at higher SOCs, while at lower SOC. the behaviour of the cell differs in terms of terminal voltage due to the charging and discharging current. This is attributed to the changes in the internal resistance of the cell. Fig. 4 illustrates the curve-fitting plot of the estimated data using the 5th-degree polynomial fitting method described in the previous section. As demonstrated in Tables 3 and 4, and the graphs in Fig. 5 , the polynomial method (1st to 8th degree) is used as a basis for fitting and estimating the
V OCV
and R0 of the cell.
Also, in Table 6 , the results of the evaluation of the model with the adopted polynomial method are shown.

Fig. 5 displays battery terminal voltage calculated using the proposed methods in both static and dynamic modes. Model performance is assessed on Panasonic dataset cycles, validating accuracy. Results also incorporate data from other databases and NASA for broader battery behaviour understanding.

The output data shows a smoother curve due to filtering, closely aligning with input data along the voltage profile. This demonstrates the model's satisfactory performance in predicting battery behaviour using experimental data. Additionally, to evaluate the performance of the model under varying conditions, the root mean square (RMS) values of the output voltage for each method are presented in Table 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Software results and discussion on HABPE method
ce:section.ce:para: Data from graphs and Table 7 demonstrate the tool's simple mode. This article proposes a hybrid mode using current profiles to extract the ECM and estimate parameters. Results for static, emotional, and fluctuating current profiles using dynamic mode are compared. Dynamic model outperforms in identifying the equivalent electrical model and accuracy. Fig. 6 provides illustrative insights based on battery terminal voltage data and Table 7 analysis.

The static mode is more suitable for accurately estimating battery terminal voltage when encountering significant input/output current variations, while the dynamic mode is preferred for precise accuracy under typical conditions. The hybrid mode (HABPE) integrates battery current profiles, architectural considerations, and optimal estimation modes. Notably, the dynamic method surpasses the static one in terminal voltage estimation, with both modes performing comparably in smoother current profiles. The proposed system achieves 82 % accuracy in static mode and 87 % in dynamic mode. Utilizing MATLAB R2021b on a system equipped with a Core i5 processor, 16GB RAM, and GTX 1650 graphics, the static and dynamic models require 72–90 s and 380–450 s, respectively, occupying 40–65 % of RAM. The software outputs polynomial coefficients and re-estimated curve-fitted data, with Simscape/MATLAB generating the battery's first-order model.

In summary, this article presents a straightforward technique to acquire equivalent battery parameters for research and modelling purposes. Utilizing linear fitting of voltage-current data, it extracts open-circuit voltage and internal resistance. Curve fitting is then applied to construct the voltage and internal resistance behaviour diagram. The resultant simulation block serves as the battery's equivalent circuit model. Notably, the method offers user-friendly steps yielding a reasonably accurate model that visually bridges initial battery data to the final predictive model. Furthermore, the study introduces the crucial idea of selecting an optimal extraction method for the battery model while ensuring the model closely mirrors actual battery behaviour. The main achievements of this research are:
•
Simplified Parameter Extraction: Equivalence battery parameters are obtained using basic test data, eliminating the need for complex impedance equipment.
•
High Accuracy: Achieves about 85% accuracy in estimating essential battery parameters, adaptable to various test conditions and equivalent models.
•
User-Friendly Model Creation: A simple interface generates a final battery model for behaviour simulation based on real-world data.
•
Adaptive Refinement: The model adapts to unique data conditions and current trends, enhancing accuracy via the Hybrid Adaptive Battery Parameter Estimation (HABPE) mode.

It should be noted that this article made the simplifying assumption of not considering the second order of the battery model and removing its dynamic behaviour. In a word, the final and complementary idea of this research is to provide a solution for extracting the battery equivalent circuit without the need for a custom method for each battery and any condition, and finally to provide an extraction model with high accuracy in the research of this field. Future research could explore more complex battery models and consider the effects of temperature, health level, and other factors in obtaining battery parameters.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper
